posts.name: Alex Goldstein
posts.cooked:
GRANDS ECHEZEAUX - (10/26/2018)

Champagne & White Burgundy

  • 1996 Dom Perignon Champagne Rosé P2 - France, Champagne
    Zippy acidity, soft strawberry attack, and rhubarb on finish. I liked this, the group was going totally gaga for it, but I was wondering how much of that was the label. Delicious though it may be, I didn’t think the quality was commensurate with current market price - a great NV Krug Rose or a Dom Ruinart Rose just as good or better. The '96 Dom Oenotheque Brut (blanc) was a more interesting wine, and less than half the price. C’est la guerre…
  • 1995 Kalin Cellars Chardonnay Cuvée CH - USA, California, Sonoma County, Sonoma Coast
    I had a lot of fun with this one, sneaking it into a Burgundy tasting. The color was dark gold, but the fruit was totally in tact, with hazelnut on the finish. A dense wine. The guesses from these experienced Burgundy drinkers: Montrachet, Corton Charlemagne, and Batard Montrachet. Most guesses on vintage were close to the mark, with '92 being the consensus. Bottom line is this is a unicorn, I can’t think of any other California Chardonnay I’ve had that’s aged like this – I hope and wish there are more wines like this coming from the region. I’ve had other '95 Chardonnay wines from Kalin, and I liked them too, but this CH is the best of them. At current pricing, an absolute steal. Only caveat would be drink them fairly soon, not sure how long they’ll remain at this level.
  • 1999 Bouchard Père et Fils Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Montrachet Grand Cru
    Served blind. Dense, rich, more mineral than the previous wine (which turned out to be a California ringer, '95 Kalin CH). Some sweettart on the midpalate and a touch of astringency on the finish. Guesses were slim all around the table, we settled on Leroy Meursault.
  • 2005 Henri Boillot Meursault 1er Cru Les Perrières - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Meursault 1er Cru
    Served blind. Classical in comparison to the previous two wines ('95 Kalin CH and '99 Bouchard Montrachet), more tension and acidity. Slight oak on the finish, and chalky minerality becomes quite prominent with air.
  • 2005 Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey Bâtard-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Bâtard-Montrachet Grand Cru
    This was a day of trickery, with my California ringer and now being served two bottles of the same wine side by side. The wines were so different, not one person at the table identified them as being the same. One was prematurely oxidized, and the other balanced with superb minerality. My notes:

  1. Very dark color, another person calls oxidation to me it’s drinkable with fruit. I’m guessing this is a Leflaive that’s starting to turn, a high breed wine that’s not showing as well as it could.

  2. Smoky/flinty nose with lemony palate, huge minerality on the finish. Guessing Coche Meursault or PYCM Meursault.

So there it is. Stump the chumps indeed! Too bad this wine suffers from so many prematurely oxidized bottles, because the good bottles are still young.

  • 2001 Maison Leroy Meursault 1er Cru Les Perrières - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Meursault 1er Cru
    Served blind. Dark color, lemon, seems simple to me but others at the table raving. Guessed Meursault but was uncertain about producer, figured it was 01-04 maybe showing a bit older than a perfect bottle should.
  • 2010 Faiveley Bâtard-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Bâtard-Montrachet Grand Cru
    Served blind. So young, so oaky. Lot of fresh pear. Stood out like a sore thumb after all these mature white wines.

Grands Echezeaux

  • 2004 Gros Frère et Sœur Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind, we only knew the theme (Grands Echezeaux) and that we were going from young to old. The age was confounding, it certainly didn’t drink very young, seemed at peak maturity. A gorgeous aroma, very sexy. Brownish red color. Flowers, black cherry in spades, and a touch of funk. Mouthfeel is amazing, I love this wine.
  • 2002 Gros Frère et Sœur Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. Showing much younger than first wine ('04 Gros GE), and I prefer that first wine. This is too woody at present, and a touch acidic on the finish. Just too darn young right now.

Upon the reveal I was surprised, as I recall having had this wine earlier this year and it was glorious. Maybe this wine was stored in very cold cellar and more backward?

  • 1992 Mongeard-Mugneret Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. Ooh la la, this is softer and sexier than the first two wines. Flowers, black cherry, and a hint of soap. The aroma here is equal to a fine vintage of DRC GE, it’s exceptional. I guessed 2000 vintage, this is in very fine shape indeed.
  • 1996 Mongeard-Mugneret Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. So soft, this is velvet. Dark color, intense, and a slightly more rustic palate that seems on the young side. I guessed '99.
  • 1990 Mongeard-Mugneret Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. Fair amount of bricking, very very pretty wine with enticing aromas. This is slightly acidic, but oh so sweet on the midpalate. I love it.

Correctly guessed the wine to boot, it’s very memorable. Always a treat to have a well stored bottle of this special wine.

  • 1993 Mongeard-Mugneret Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. VA and/or heat damage, it was undrinkable to all at the table. NR (flawed)
  • 1982 Mongeard-Mugneret Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. This was a confounding wine, so much material here ruined by TCA. It wasn’t undrinkable, the TCA was quite minor at first. If not for this flaw, would have showed brilliantly – some thought it would have been wine of the day. Cork aside, it’s a testament to the ageworthiness of MM’s GE. There was a polish and presence to the wine that was remarkable, would love to taste a sound bottle of this wine sometime.
  • 1959 Georges Noellat Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. Massive TCA, what a shame I love Georges Noellat. NR (flawed)
  • 1940 Domaine de la Romanée-Conti Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    This World War II era wine certainly looked like it had been through a war, the label was tattered. The wine came from a generous friend who is a longtime Burgundy collector, so we are confident this is the genuine article so to speak. The color was light, a browning garnet that was closer to transparent than opaque. The wine was somewhat acrid, and never really came around. There was the occasional enticing aroma, and some moments of hope that it would stretch its legs and come around, though it never did. It’s an honor to taste something of this age and historical significance, and I am grateful for the opportunity.
  • 2005 Mongeard-Mugneret Grands-Echezeaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
    Served blind. Gingerbread aroma, young very dark color. This seems way young with lots of material to develop, correctly guessed '05.

Afterparty

  • 2001 Domaine Ramonet Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Montrachet Grand Cru
    Gorgeous wine, chiseled and lithe. Some thought it to be more advanced than it “should” be, but that seemed like nitpicking. A serious and impressive wine in its prime. I only wish we had it earlier in the night, palate fatigue had set in by the time this and other gonzo wines were served.
  • 2004 Domaine Ramonet Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Montrachet Grand Cru
    Served blind and the aroma had everyone under its spell. Flowers and an ethereal “something” that beckons. Palate was less giving than the aroma and when it was revealed we learned why. This magnificent wine deserves to be followed for hours. Majestic and we are just scratching the surface.
  • 2004 Domaine Ramonet Bâtard-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Bâtard-Montrachet Grand Cru
    Served blind some liked this more than the Montrachet. I thought it was more accessible out of the gate but the aroma not quite as exciting. Upon the reveal this too made sense, it’s showing better out of the gate. Monty is better, but this is pretty darn close in quality, just a touch fatter and less aromatic. Excellent Batard.
  • 2013 Jean-Marie Fourrier Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Amoureuses - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru
    From magnum, this was a dark and exotic spice bomb. Serious fireworks on the nose, spicy and sexy. The palate was very muted by comparison, and I didn’t have enough time to let it develop. Obviously this was opened way too young, especially in magnum and to pop and pour with no decant. Nevertheless, it’s evident it will be a stellar wine in time. The aromatics alone are worth experiencing.

posts.name: Barry Rothof
posts.cooked: Great wines and notes ! Thx for sharing

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: Wow, I’d be happy to sit thru the pre or post parties and wines!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Nice tasting! That mm GE is still pretty affordable too, I see 12s at around 150.

posts.name: p@ulbortin
posts.cooked: Love that 2001 Monty. Thanks for the report.

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked:
It is a pity that the Ramonet wines came afterwards. Probably should have been drunk at the start and given time to open up.

I have been more impressed by Mongeard Mugneret Echezeaux VV than their Grands Echezeaux when tasted side by side.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
It is a pity that the Ramonet wines came afterwards. Probably should have been drunk at the start and given time to open up.

I have been more impressed by Mongeard Mugneret Echezeaux VV than their Grands Echezeaux when tasted side by side.

Like both MMS, but like you sneaking preference for the Echezeaux. Always afraid, people will get to know how good these wines are, and prices will do an Engel or Truchot.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Well they don’t make the ech VV anymore.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked:
Well they don’t make the ech VV anymore.

now they make The Grand Complication ech (probably spelt a little differently), it come in 3 bottle cases. I bought it in 14 and 15, they didnt make it in 16.
I believe the VV vines were gifted to a Hospice

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Anyone ever try the MM 2000 ech VV?

posts.name: Thomas De Waen
posts.cooked:
Anyone ever try the MM 2000 ech VV?

Yes. It rocks. Aromatics are fabulous. Picked up a case at auction for a song… still a relatively undiscovered domaine…

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked:
Well they don’t make the ech VV anymore.

Yes, now gone (donated ?) to Hospice…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Nice notes. Love the 1995 Kalin CH, along with Ted Lemon’s wine of the same vintage, the first ever vineyard designate from Charles Heintz ranch.

My last 2005 Morey Bâtard was premoxed too, alas.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: Ramonet 04s are real stars. Lots of good stuff in that tasting. Thanks.

posts.name: Alex Goldstein
posts.cooked:
That was the second oxidized bottle of 05 PYCM Batard in one month. Quite frustrating given how excellent the wine is when it’s sound.

Nice notes. Love the 1995 Kalin CH, along with Ted Lemon’s wine of the same vintage, the first ever vineyard designate from Charles Heintz ranch.

My last 2005 Morey Bâtard was premoxed too, alas.
